text: This robotic video highlights a total pelvic accentuation with a caloanal anastomosis and an intra-assisted plaster urological reconstruction. We report the case of a fifty-nine-year-old male diagnosed with locally advanced mid-rectal cancer involving the urogenital apparatus, staged as a MRT-IV-BN-II-EMVI-positive. He was treated with induction chemotherapy with full sarcosophageal fironauts followed by chemoradiotherapy prior to undertaking a robotic total pelvic accentuation with coloanal anastomosis and endorhizoplasty.

The robotic pelvic accentuation begins transanally with a full thickness rectotomy made approximately one centimeter proximal to the dentate line. The surgeon enters the TME plane posteriorly from the transanal approach, continuing dissection down to the pelvic floor to connect with previous transanal dissection. The dissection proceeds circumferentially along the right and left lateral TME planes. The left ureter is identified and dissected from the pelvic brim to the vesico-ureteric junction, ensuring a cuff of tissue is maintained around it to preserve vascular integrity for the ileo-ureteric anastomosis.

The surgeon identifies and dissects the right ureter as it crosses the bifurcation of the common iliac artery, ensuring a cuff of tissue remains around it. The urinary bladder is mobilized anteriorly, and the pre-vesicle space is opened, revealing the pubic bone. The previously dissected left ureter is visible, and transection is performed once the bladder is fully mobilized to achieve maximum ureter length for anastomosis.

The surgeon continues bladder mobilization laterally and inferiorly, reaching the endopelvic fascia. An energy device is used to divide the inferior and superior vesicle vascular pedicles. The left vas deferens is visible and divided using the robotic energy device. The endopelvic fascia is seen reflecting off the levator ani muscle.

The surgeon transects the right ureter near the vesico-ureteric junction and mobilizes the right lateral bladder. The endopelvic fascia is incised, and the prostate is mobilized. The urethra is identified, and a urethrotomy is performed, dividing the urinary catheter. The dorsal venous complex is sutured to achieve hemostasis. The dissection is confirmed with transanal digital examination.
The surgeon exteriorizes the previously mobilized colon through the anus, with approximately five centimeters of the colon visible outside the body to facilitate a delayed coloanal anastomosis.

The surgeon performs a robotic urological enterocysteoplasty, isolating a 30 cm ileal segment approximately 20 cm from the ileocecal valve. An enterotomy is made, and the posterior wall of an ileourethral anastomosis is constructed. The proximal and distal segments of the ileal segment are transected with a stapler and detubularized. The small bowel segment is opened along its anti-mesenteric border to the ileourethral anastomosis. GI continuity is restored with a side-to-side stapled anastomosis using two firings of the robotic stapler. The detubularized ileal segments are sutured to form the posterior wall of the neobladder, folding the corners to create a spherical shape. A self-locking barbed suture is used to construct the neobladder, with the anterior aspect formed by suturing the lateral aspects of the ileal segments, completing the anterior wall of the ileourethral anastomosis.

The spherical shape of the neobladder is visibly forming as the superior aspect is closed. An enterotomy is made in the corner of the neobladder, followed by an end-to-end ileo-ureteric anastomosis with a double J stent inserted to protect the anastomosis. The previously dissected left ureter is located, and a second double J stent is robotically placed into the left ureter and the neobladder pouch.

The urological anastomosis is complete and we're left having performed a total pelvic accentuation with a coloanal anastomosis and a urological enterocystopasty reconstruction of the bladder. Our total operating time was seven hours with a three hundred millilitre blood loss. Postoperatively, the patient was enrolled into our pelvic accentuation enhanced recovery programme and had an event for recovery. His histology was a T-for-B-N-naught-R-naught resection. At three months' follow-up, he had recovered well from his operation, had a last score of ten, and had no urinary symptoms.
